what_it_does: A static, single-file HTML design board that presents the 'pilot mode' interaction model for a unified human/AI assistant interface, listing current modes, spatial perspectives, built primitives, guiding principles, and open design questions.
observations: Dark, responsive, single-file HTML with embedded CSS and no JavaScript
Uses CSS custom properties, clamp() typography, and auto-fit CSS grids
A hero equation frames the user as pilot, the engine as generative, the interface as space, and the activity as flying
Three main modes: Pilot (human steers), Autopilot (engine runs), and future modes to imagine
Three perspective metaphors: Cockpit (first-person), Chase (third-person), Orbital (top-down)
Cards are tagged with status labels: built, next, idea, principle
Built primitives include a stability dial, anticipation dial, projection instrument, and a 64-tool verb palette
Open questions are grouped under pilot interaction, space & movement, and underlying cognition
The page states that the user and the engine are both projections of one substrate and share the same tools
standouts: Pilot mode is framed as the user choosing each tool call while the engine proposes and asks
The stability dial is explicitly the same spectrum as pilot ↔ autopilot
The same 64-verb tool palette is meant to belong to both the user and the engine
'The data itself is the screen' is called out as a core principle
The file is a conceptual design artifact, not a running interactive prototype
conventions: HTML5 single-page document with all styles inline in a <style> block
Semantic, hyphenated class names and CSS custom properties for theming
Responsive layout via auto-fit grid and clamp() font sizes
Status tags rendered as small uppercase labels with color-coded borders
Emoji used as card icons; no external images or scripts
Some tags use inline style overrides for positioning
concerns: Inline style overrides on tags may complicate maintenance
No semantic landmarks such as <main>, <section>, or <footer>
Abstract terms like 'projection instrument', 'one substrate', and 'verb palette' are not defined technically here
No accessibility annotations (aria-label, alt text, focus indicators)
Static only: the interactive palette and dials described are not implemented in this file
notes: A static, dark-themed design board HTML that lays out the 'pilot mode' interaction model and open questions for a unified human+AI tool interface.
questions: What exactly is the 'projection instrument' / radial lens-wheel and how does it resolve meaning into points?
What are the 64 tools in the verb palette and how are they exposed to the user?
How are the stability and anticipation dials implemented and surfaced in the UI?
What does 'you and it are both projections of the one substrate' mean in system terms?
How does perspective switching (cockpit/chase/orbital) translate to concrete navigation gestures or controls?
What is the data model of 'the one space' that the interface projects?
How does the user choose or redirect a tool call mid-flight without breaking context?
purpose_vs_actual: Yes — the file presents itself as a living ideas board for pilot mode and future modes, and it does exactly that through sections for modes, perspectives, primitives, and open questions.
apparent_themes: Shared tool space where user and engine use the same verbs
Spatial/aviation metaphor for interaction (pilot, autopilot, cockpit, chase, orbital)
Modes as a registry of postures over one substrate
intention_signals: To define and socialize a product interaction model in which the user steers an AI engine along an autonomy spectrum, using shared tools and spatial perspective metaphors, while separating already-built primitives from still-open design questions.
novelty: The aviation-style pilot/autopilot framing combined with a shared user/engine verb palette, a 'data itself is the screen' projection principle, and camera-like perspective modes over a single semantic space.
contribution: Contributes the conceptual interaction vocabulary (pilot/autopilot, stability/anticipation dials, cockpit/chase/orbital perspectives, shared verb palette, data-as-screen principle), a structured inventory of built primitives, and an explicit set of open design questions that a fused system would keep as requirements and conversation anchors.
summary_for_embedding: A static HTML design board titled 'Pilot mode & the modes to come' that outlines a human-in-the-loop 'pilot' interaction model, an autonomy spectrum from pilot to autopilot, spatial perspectives, shared user/engine tools, existing primitives, and open questions for a unified AI assistant interface.
intention_for_embedding: To capture and communicate the pilot-mode product vision, distinguish built primitives from open design questions, and align the team before building the interactive tool face.
